abs.description: Returns the absolute value of arg. If arg is an integer, returns an integer; if arg is a floating-point number, returns a floating-point number.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: A numeric value (integer or floating point).

abs.example:
set a [expr {abs(-3.14)}]   ;# returns 3.14
set b [expr {abs(-5)}]      ;# returns 5

acos.description: Returns the arc cosine of arg, in the range [0, pi] radians. arg must be in the range [-1, 1].

asin.description: Returns the arc sine of arg, in the range [-pi/2, pi/2] radians. arg must be in the range [-1, 1].

atan.description: Returns the arc tangent of arg, in the range [-pi/2, pi/2] radians.

atan2.description: Returns the four-quadrant arc tangent of y/x, in the range [-pi, pi] radians. x and y cannot both be 0. Uses the signs of both arguments to determine the correct quadrant.
atan2.example:
set angle [expr {atan2(1.0, 0.0)}]   ;# returns approx 1.5708 (pi/2)
set angle [expr {atan2(-1.0, -1.0)}] ;# returns approx -2.3562 (-3pi/4)

bool.description: Returns 0 if arg is zero or the string "false", otherwise 1. Also accepts "true" as true.
bool.example:
set b1 [expr {bool(0)}]            ;# returns 0
set b2 [expr {bool(42)}]           ;# returns 1
set b3 [expr {bool("false")}]      ;# returns 0

ceil.description: Returns the smallest integer value not less than arg, as a floating-point number (with zero fractional part). This is ceiling (round up).
ceil.example:
set c1 [expr {ceil(3.14)}]   ;# returns 4.0
set c2 [expr {ceil(-3.14)}]  ;# returns -3.0
set c3 [expr {ceil(5.0)}]    ;# returns 5.0

cos.description: Returns the cosine of arg, measured in radians.
cos.example:
set val [expr {cos(0.0)}]        ;# returns 1.0
set val [expr {cos(3.14159/2)}] ;# returns approx 0.0

cosh.description: Returns the hyperbolic cosine of arg. Results may overflow for large arguments, causing an error.
cosh.example:
set val [expr {cosh(0.0)}]  ;# returns 1.0
set val [expr {cosh(1.0)}]  ;# returns approx 1.5431

double.description: Converts arg to a double-precision floating-point number. If arg is an integer that exceeds the range of double, returns Inf or -Inf.
double.example:
set d1 [expr {double(42)}]     ;# returns 42.0
set d2 [expr {double(3)}]      ;# returns 3.0

entier.description: Returns the integer part of arg (truncation toward zero). Unlike int() and wide(), the result has unlimited integer range. Introduced in Tcl 8.5 to replace implicit truncation behavior.
entier.example:
set e1 [expr {entier(3.99)}]   ;# returns 3
set e2 [expr {entier(-3.99)}]  ;# returns -3

exp.description: Returns e raised to the power of arg (the exponential function). Results may overflow, causing an error.
exp.example:
set val [expr {exp(0.0)}]   ;# returns 1.0
set val [expr {exp(1.0)}]   ;# returns approx 2.7183

floor.description: Returns the largest integer value not greater than arg, as a floating-point number (with zero fractional part). This is floor (round down).
floor.example:
set f1 [expr {floor(3.14)}]   ;# returns 3.0
set f2 [expr {floor(-3.14)}]  ;# returns -4.0

fmod.description: Returns the floating-point remainder of x divided by y. If y is 0, an error is raised.
fmod.example:
set r1 [expr {fmod(10.0, 3.0)}]  ;# returns 1.0
set r2 [expr {fmod(7.5, 2.0)}]   ;# returns 1.5

hypot.description: Returns the length of the hypotenuse of a right triangle with sides x and y: sqrt(x*x + y*y). More numerically stable than manual computation, avoiding intermediate overflow or underflow.
hypot.example:
set h [expr {hypot(3.0, 4.0)}]  ;# returns 5.0
set h [expr {hypot(1e200, 1e200)}] ;# avoids overflow, returns approx 1.414e200

int.description: Returns the integer part of arg by truncation toward zero. The result is limited to the machine word size (typically 32 or 64 bits). For unlimited integer range, use entier() instead.
int.example:
set i1 [expr {int(3.99)}]   ;# returns 3
set i2 [expr {int(-3.99)}]  ;# returns -3

isqrt.description: Returns the integer part of the square root of arg. arg must be a non-negative integer. Unlike sqrt(), the result is an arbitrary-precision integer. Introduced in Tcl 8.5.
isqrt.example:
set i [expr {isqrt(16)}]    ;# returns 4
set i [expr {isqrt(20)}]    ;# returns 4

log.description: Returns the natural logarithm (base e) of arg. arg must be a positive value.
log.example:
set val [expr {log(1.0)}]    ;# returns 0.0
set val [expr {log(2.7183)}] ;# returns approx 1.0

log10.description: Returns the base-10 logarithm of arg. arg must be a positive value.
log10.example:
set val [expr {log10(100.0)}]  ;# returns 2.0
set val [expr {log10(1.0)}]    ;# returns 0.0

max.description: Returns the maximum value among all its arguments. Accepts one or more numeric arguments. If exactly one argument is given, returns that argument. The return type matches the input type (integer or floating-point).

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: One or more numeric values to compare.

max.example:
set m1 [expr {max(3, 7, 2, 9)}]   ;# returns 9
set m2 [expr {max(-1.5, -2.0)}]  ;# returns -1.5

min.description: Returns the minimum value among all its arguments. Accepts one or more numeric arguments. If exactly one argument is given, returns that argument.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: One or more numeric values to compare.

min.example:
set m1 [expr {min(3, 7, 2, 9)}]   ;# returns 2
set m2 [expr {min(-1.5, -2.0)}]  ;# returns -2.0

pow.description: Returns x raised to the power y. If x is negative, y must be an integer value. Results may overflow.

parameters.name: y
parameters.desc: The exponent. Must be integral if x is negative.

pow.example:
set p1 [expr {pow(2.0, 10.0)}]   ;# returns 1024.0
set p2 [expr {pow(9.0, 0.5)}]    ;# returns 3.0 (square root)

rand.description: Returns a pseudo-random floating-point number in the range (0, 1). The random number generator is a simple linear congruential generator and is NOT cryptographically secure. The seed is initialized from the system clock. Use srand() to set a specific seed.
rand.example:
set r [expr {rand()}]         ;# e.g., 0.5321
set scaled [expr {int(rand() * 100)}] ;# random integer 0-99

round.description: Rounds arg to the nearest integer value. If arg is exactly halfway between two integers, rounds to the nearest even integer (banker's rounding). If arg is already an integer, returns it unchanged.
round.example:
set r1 [expr {round(3.4)}]   ;# returns 3
set r2 [expr {round(3.5)}]   ;# returns 4
set r3 [expr {round(2.5)}]   ;# returns 2

sin.description: Returns the sine of arg, measured in radians.
sin.example:
set val [expr {sin(0.0)}]        ;# returns 0.0
set val [expr {sin(3.14159/2)}] ;# returns approx 1.0

sinh.description: Returns the hyperbolic sine of arg. Results may overflow for large arguments, causing an error.
sinh.example:
set val [expr {sinh(0.0)}]  ;# returns 0.0
set val [expr {sinh(1.0)}]  ;# returns approx 1.1752

sqrt.description: Returns the square root of arg. arg must be non-negative. May return Inf for very large arguments.
sqrt.example:
set s1 [expr {sqrt(16.0)}]  ;# returns 4.0
set s2 [expr {sqrt(2.0)}]   ;# returns approx 1.4142

srand.description: Seeds the random number generator used by rand() with the integer arg. The seed is normally initialized from the system clock. Returns the first random number of the new sequence (same as calling rand() immediately after srand).

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: The seed value for the random number generator.

srand.example:
expr {srand(42)}        ;# seed with 42, returns first random
set r [expr {rand()}]    ;# next random value

tan.description: Returns the tangent of arg, measured in radians.
tan.example:
set val [expr {tan(0.0)}]        ;# returns 0.0
set val [expr {tan(3.14159/4)}] ;# returns approx 1.0

tanh.description: Returns the hyperbolic tangent of arg. The result is always in the range (-1, 1).
tanh.example:
set val [expr {tanh(0.0)}]  ;# returns 0.0
set val [expr {tanh(10.0)}] ;# returns approx 1.0

wide.description: Converts arg to a 64-bit integer (equivalent to C's long long type). The value is truncated toward zero. For unlimited integer range, use entier() instead.
wide.example:
set w [expr {wide(3.99)}]   ;# returns 3
set w [expr {wide(2.0 ** 40)}] ;# returns 1099511627776
